memes.label: Dramatis Personae
memes.description: "List of characters in Macbeth"

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: Alarum
memes.description: The Shakespearean stage direction "alarum" — a trumpet or drum call signalling battle, alarm, or military action — and its symbolic use in Macbeth at moments of murder and cosmic disorder (010-034, 025-015).

memes.label: Ambition
memes.description: The driving force of Macbeth's tragic trajectory—ambition that overleaps moral bounds and consumes its possessor.

memes.label: Ambition & Power
memes.description: An index of all pages exploring ambition, power, and kingship in Macbeth — the central thematic complex that drives the tragedy from prophecy to tyranny.

memes.label: Angel
memes.description: Angelic imagery in Macbeth as a figure of divine witness and judgment against the murder of Duncan.

memes.label: Angus
memes.description: A Scottish nobleman loyal to King Duncan and later to Malcolm, present throughout the play as witness to Macbeth's rise and fall.

memes.label: Apparitions
memes.description: The three prophetic visions conjured by the Weird Sisters in Act IV, Scene I that give Macbeth false confidence through deliberately deceptive predictions.

memes.label: Armour
memes.description: The motif of armour and arming in Macbeth, concentrated in Act V Scene III where Macbeth's three demands for his armour expose his deteriorating psychological state as Dunsinane is besieged (023-025, 023-027, 023-031).

memes.label: Avarice
memes.description: The insatiable greed that Malcolm claims as a vice worse than Macbeth's tyranny in Act IV Scene iii—a strategic self-slander designed to test Macduff's loyalty.

memes.label: Banquet
memes.description: The banquet as a symbol of social order and hospitality in Macbeth, violently disrupted when Banquo's ghost appears at the royal feast, exposing Macbeth's guilt and shattered authority.

memes.label: Banquo
memes.description: A Scottish thane and Macbeth's comrade-in-arms whose descendants are prophesied to inherit the throne. Banquo serves as Macbeth's moral foil — he too receives a supernatural prophecy, but refuses to act on it through violence, embodying the path of restraint that Macbeth abandons.

memes.label: Banquo's Murder
memes.description: 'The assassination of Banquo by Macbeth''s hired murderers in Act III, Scene III; Fleance escapes, preserving the witches'' prophecy of Banquo''s royal lineage (014-016).'

memes.label: Bat
memes.description: The bat in Macbeth as a symbol of nocturnal evil and a key ingredient in the witches' cauldron.

memes.label: Battle of Dunsinane
memes.description: The climactic battle of Shakespeare's Macbeth, in which Malcolm's English-backed army besieges Dunsinane Castle and defeats Macbeth's forces, restoring legitimate rule to Scotland.

memes.label: Bear
memes.description: The bear as metaphor for Macbeth's final stand — the baited animal fighting a losing battle against overwhelming odds.

memes.label: Bell
memes.description: The bell as a death signal in Macbeth — from Lady Macbeth's summoning bell to the alarum bell of battle, the ringing that marks every fatal transition in the play.

memes.label: Bird
memes.description: Avian imagery in Macbeth — birds as omens, symbols of natural order disrupted, and metaphors for vulnerability and predation.

memes.label: Birnam Wood
memes.description: A forest near Dunsinane in Perthshire, Scotland, central to the witches' prophecy that Macbeth cannot be vanquished until Birnam Wood comes to Dunsinane.

memes.label: Birnam Wood Marches
memes.description: The fulfilment of the Witches' third apparition, as Malcolm's army advances on Dunsinane camouflaged by branches cut from Birnam Wood.

memes.label: Blood
memes.description: The most visceral symbolic medium in Macbeth — blood marks guilt, stains conscience, and traces the play's arc from battlefield honour to murder to tyranny to retribution.

memes.label: Blood as Motif
memes.description: Tracing blood as the unifying visual motif of Macbeth — from battlefield honour through murder and guilt to irreversible moral habituation. Blood transforms across the play from a mark of valour into an indelible stain of conscience, mapping the tragedy's moral arc.

memes.label: Bubble
memes.description: The image of "bubble" in Macbeth — from Banquo's observation that "the earth hath bubbles" to the witches' cauldron refrain — capturing the insubstantiality of supernatural appearances and the fragility of Macbeth's power.

memes.label: By Quality
memes.description: A quality-tier index of all entry pages in the Macbeth wiki, grouped by quality level — from featured flagship pages through standard entries to basic stubs.

memes.label: Caithness
memes.description: A Scottish thane who joins the rebel forces against Macbeth in Act V, providing insight into the tyrant's deteriorating mental state.

memes.label: Candle
memes.description: Macbeth's metaphor of life as a brief candle, connecting the play's light/darkness imagery to existential nihilism.

memes.label: Captain (Bleeding Captain)
memes.description: A wounded soldier who reports Macbeth's battlefield valour to King Duncan in Act I, Scene II, framing the audience's first impression of the protagonist through the lens of martial heroism.

memes.label: Cauldron
memes.description: The witches' cauldron in Act IV Scene i as the symbolic centre of supernatural agency in Macbeth—a vessel of transformation where prophecy, poison, and deceptive truth are brewed together.

memes.label: Cawdor
memes.description: Cawdor in Shakespeare's Macbeth.

memes.label: Chaos
memes.description: The systematic disintegration of natural, political, and psychological order in Macbeth, triggered by Duncan's murder and spreading outward from the royal chamber to the cosmos itself.

memes.label: Characters
memes.description: A comprehensive index of all characters appearing in Shakespeare's Macbeth, grouped by significance and allegiance.

memes.label: Charm
memes.description: The motif of charms and spellcraft in Macbeth—from the witches' "charm's wound up" to Macbeth's fatal trust in his "charmed life"—tracing how magical language shapes belief and leads to destruction.

memes.label: Child (Children and Lineage)
memes.description: The child motif in Macbeth centres on the play's preoccupation with succession, inheritance, and the future — from the witches' prophecy that Banquo's children will be kings to the murdered Macduff children and the apparition of the "blood-bolter'd" child who heralds Macduff's unique power over Macbeth.

memes.label: Colmekill
memes.description: The sacred burial place of Scottish kings, mentioned in Macbeth as the resting place of King Duncan's body after his murder.

memes.label: Concepts
memes.description: A comprehensive index of all concepts, themes, motifs, and abstract ideas in the Macbeth wiki — from ambition and guilt to the supernatural and the natural order, organized by category and cross-referenced by significance.

memes.label: Conscience
memes.description: The faculty of moral self-awareness in Macbeth, explored through its suppression, its return as hallucination, and its ultimate failure to prevent catastrophic violence.

memes.label: Courage
memes.description: The contested virtue of courage in Macbeth, where physical bravery on the battlefield becomes entangled with moral cowardice in the chamber, and Lady Macbeth weaponises the idea of courage to goad her husband to murder.

memes.label: Crow
memes.description: The crow and raven as death-omens in Macbeth — bird imagery signalling the inversion of natural order and the approach of murder.

memes.label: Crown
memes.description: The physical and symbolic object of Macbeth's ambition—the crown represents legitimate rule, usurped authority, and the burden of illegitimate power.

memes.label: Cry (Lamentation)
memes.description: The recurring motif of the cry — of pain, grief, alarm, and battle — that punctuates Macbeth's soundscape, from the wounded soldier's gashes to the "cry of women" announcing Lady Macbeth's death.

memes.label: Curse
memes.description: The power of the spoken curse in Macbeth — from the witches' maledictions and Macbeth's self-cursing to the community's final condemnation of the tyrant as "accursed."

memes.label: Dagger
memes.description: The instrument of Duncan's murder and the focus of Macbeth's most famous hallucination—the dagger is the play's central symbol of the threshold between thought and violent action.

memes.label: Darkness
memes.description: The pervasive imagery of darkness in Macbeth — from the "thick night" Lady Macbeth invokes to the unnatural dark that follows Duncan's murder. Darkness is the play's visual signature for evil, secrecy, and the collapse of moral vision.

memes.label: Darkness and Evil
memes.description: The moral identification of darkness with evil in Macbeth, where characters invoke hell's darkness to enable their crimes and the play traces the progressive darkening of the human soul.

memes.label: Death
memes.description: The ever-present horizon of action in Macbeth—death as political instrument, psychological torment, and ultimate moral reckoning.

memes.label: Death & Violence
memes.description: An index of all pages exploring death, violence, and murder in Macbeth — from Duncan's assassination to the final battlefield, tracing how Shakespeare renders mortality as both political act and metaphysical horror.

memes.label: Deed
memes.description: The act that transforms thought into irreversible reality—the central mechanism of moral consequence in Macbeth.

memes.label: Despair
memes.description: The condition of utter hopelessness in Macbeth, manifesting both as Macbeth's nihilistic abandonment of meaning and as a spiritual sickness that only divine grace can cure.

memes.label: Devil
memes.description: Diabolical language and imagery in Macbeth — the devil as metaphor, personification, and final identity of the tragic protagonist.

memes.label: Disease and Healing
memes.description: The recurring metaphor of disease in Macbeth, where Scotland is depicted as a sick body under a diseased king, contrasted with the healing powers of the English monarch.

memes.label: Divine Right of Kings
memes.description: The Jacobean political doctrine that kings derive authority directly from God — the theological framework that makes Macbeth's regicide not merely treason but sacrilege.

memes.label: The Scottish Doctor
memes.description: A Scottish physician who observes Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking and diagnoses her condition as a spiritual rather than physical malady, later attending Macbeth at Dunsinane and bearing witness to the collapse of the Scottish body politic.

memes.label: Dog
memes.description: The dog in Macbeth as a figure of contempt, a witches' ingredient, and the ultimate epithet for the tyrant — "hell-hound."

memes.label: Donalbain
memes.description: 'Younger son of King Duncan in Shakespeare''s Macbeth, who flees to Ireland after his father''s murder (P01-003).'

memes.label: Doubling and Duplicity
memes.description: The motif of doubling and duplicity throughout Macbeth—from the witches' "double, double" refrain to the play's structural pattern of paired scenes, doubled language, and the gap between appearance and reality.

memes.label: Drum
memes.description: The drum as the sound of military action in Macbeth — the aural signature of battle, authority, and the approach of retribution.

memes.label: Duncan
memes.description: King of Scotland at the play's opening. Duncan is the virtuous monarch whose murder at Macbeth's hands is both a political crime and a cosmic violation. His character embodies legitimate kingship, sacred hospitality, and the divine right that Macbeth's tyranny can never claim.

memes.label: Duncan's Murder
memes.description: 'The assassination of King Duncan by Macbeth at Inverness, the central crime that sets the tragedy in motion (009-005).'

memes.label: Dunsinane
memes.description: Dunsinane in Shakespeare's Macbeth.

memes.label: England
memes.description: 'The kingdom to which Malcolm flees after Duncan''s murder and from which he returns with an English army to reclaim the Scottish throne in Shakespeare''s Macbeth (010-075).'

memes.label: English Doctor
memes.description: A physician at the English court who describes King Edward the Confessor's miraculous healing touch in Act IV, Scene III (020-025), providing a structural contrast between the saintly English king and the tyrannical Macbeth.

memes.label: Equivocation
memes.description: The rhetorical and moral phenomenon of speaking truth that misleads, central to Macbeth's tragedy — from the Porter's comedy to the Weird Sisters' fatal prophecies.

memes.label: Events
memes.description: A chronological index of all key events in Shakespeare's Macbeth, from the witches' prophecy to the final battle at Dunsinane.

memes.label: Evil
memes.description: The nature and operation of evil in Macbeth — personified, supernatural, and psychological — as the central moral category of the tragedy.

memes.label: Eye
memes.description: The eye as the organ of perception and its limitations in Macbeth — seeing, not-seeing, and the horror of visual truth.

memes.label: Fair
memes.description: The concept of "fair" in Macbeth — surface appearance of goodness, beauty, and justice that masks foul reality, encapsulated in the witches' paradox "Fair is foul, and foul is fair."

memes.label: Fate
memes.description: The philosophical crux of Macbeth — does fate determine events, or does human choice? The play stages the tension between predestination and free will through the mechanism of prophecy, never resolving the question but tracing its tragic consequences.

memes.label: Fear
memes.description: The progression of fear in Macbeth — from Banquo's wary response to the Witches (003-021) through Macbeth's paranoid tyranny to Lady Macbeth's terrified sleepwalking — as the emotional engine of the play's tragic movement.

memes.label: Feast
memes.description: The feast as a symbol of kingship, hospitality, and social order in Macbeth — and its violation as the measure of tyranny.

memes.label: Fife
memes.description: A region in eastern Scotland, the thanage held by Macduff, central to the play's geography of loyalty and betrayal (002-017).

memes.label: Fleance
memes.description: 'Son of Banquo in Shakespeare''s Macbeth, whose escape from the murderers preserves the witches'' prophecy of Banquo''s royal lineage (P01-012).'

memes.label: Fleance's Escape
memes.description: The narrow escape of Banquo's son Fleance during the ambush in Act III Scene iii, a critical event that keeps the Witches' prophecy of Banquo's royal lineage alive and ensures Macbeth's dynastic failure.

memes.label: Forres
memes.description: 'The location of King Duncan''s palace in Shakespeare''s Macbeth, where the royal court convenes in the opening acts (002-001).'

memes.label: Foul
memes.description: The concept of "foul" in Macbeth — evil, disorder, and moral corruption that the witches declare interchangeable with "fair," establishing the play's central moral inversion.

memes.label: Free Will
memes.description: The tension between human choice and supernatural determination in Macbeth, explored through the question of whether the witches' prophecies cause or merely predict Macbeth's actions.

memes.label: Gate (Threshold Motif)
memes.description: The gate as a liminal symbol in Macbeth, marking the threshold between the domestic and the demonic, the private crime and public discovery.

memes.label: Gender Roles
memes.description: The play's interrogation of masculine and feminine codes — courage, violence, nurture, compassion — as characters invoke and invert gender expectations in their pursuit of power.

memes.label: Gentle
memes.description: The concept of "gentle" in Macbeth — gentleness as the quality of civilized society that regicide destroys, deployed with increasing irony as violence overtakes the social order.

memes.label: Ghost
memes.description: The supernatural manifestation of the murdered dead in Macbeth—Banquo's ghost and the apparitions conjured by the witches—as agents of guilt, prophecy, and cosmic retribution.

memes.label: Ghost of Banquo
memes.description: The apparition of Banquo's ghost at the royal banquet in Act III, Scene IV — a manifestation of Macbeth's guilt visible only to him — as the turning point where private crimes erupt into public visibility.

memes.label: Glamis
memes.description: Glamis in Shakespeare's Macbeth.

memes.label: Grace
memes.description: The theological concept of divine grace and its loss as Macbeth's soul descends into damnation, marked most famously by his inability to say "Amen."

memes.label: Grief
memes.description: Grief as a political and personal force in Macbeth — from performative mourning to Macduff's transformative grief that converts to righteous vengeance.

memes.label: Growth (Planting & Harvest Motif)
memes.description: The planting and growth metaphor in Macbeth, tracing the arc from Duncan's promise to "plant" Macbeth through to the harvest of tyranny and the renewal of legitimate rule under Malcolm.

memes.label: Guilt
memes.description: The psychology of guilt in Macbeth — from anticipatory conscience to hallucinated blood to compulsive hand-washing — tracing how Shakespeare renders guilt as a physical force that cannot be reasoned away or washed clean.

memes.label: Hand (Symbol)
memes.description: A central symbolic motif in Macbeth, representing guilt, agency, and the impossibility of washing away moral stain.

memes.label: Heart
memes.description: The heart as the seat of courage, conscience, and emotional truth in Macbeth's psychological drama.

memes.label: The Heath
memes.description: A desolate, open landscape where the Weird Sisters first encounter Macbeth and Banquo. As a liminal space between the human and supernatural worlds, the heath anchors the play's central questions of fate, free will, and the permeability of the natural order.

memes.label: Heaven (Divine Judgment)
memes.description: The motif of heaven in Macbeth functions as the absent-but-invoked source of moral order, whose silence or delayed intervention is the theological problem at the play's centre.

memes.label: Hecate
memes.description: Goddess of witchcraft in Macbeth, ruler of the three Witches, appears in Act III Scene V and Act IV Scene I.

memes.label: Hell (in Macbeth)
memes.description: Hell as invoked throughout Macbeth — a theological and symbolic location representing damnation, the Porter's comic domain, and the destiny of those who commit unnatural deeds.

memes.label: Honour
memes.description: The code of feudal loyalty and reputation that structures the moral world of Macbeth—and the standard against which its violations are measured.

memes.label: Horse
memes.description: Horses in Macbeth as symbols of natural order and its violation, from Duncan's cannibal horses to the galloping of fate.

memes.label: Host
memes.description: The sacred duty of hospitality and its violation in Macbeth, where the host becomes the murderer of his guest.

memes.label: Inverness
memes.description: 'Macbeth''s castle in Shakespeare''s tragedy, the site of King Duncan''s murder and the setting for several crucial scenes (005-001).'

memes.label: Ireland
memes.description: 'The kingdom to which Donalbain flees after his father Duncan''s murder in Shakespeare''s Macbeth (010-076).'

memes.label: Justice
memes.description: The concept of justice in Shakespeare's Macbeth — both as a principle of cosmic retribution and as a kingly virtue that Macbeth's reign systematically destroys.

memes.label: Kern
memes.description: Irish light-armed foot-soldiers who appear in *Macbeth* as mercenary forces—discussed in the opening battle report and dismissed by Macbeth in his final stand as beneath a king's steel.

memes.label: Kingship
memes.description: The concept of legitimate rule and royal authority in Macbeth, explored through the contrast between Duncan's sacred kingship and Macbeth's tyrannical usurpation.

memes.label: Knell
memes.description: The bell as a motif of death and irrevocable action in Macbeth—from the knell that summons Duncan to "heaven or to hell" to the final knell that marks Macbeth's death at Macduff's hands.

memes.label: Knocking (Gate Motif)
memes.description: The motif of knocking in Macbeth, centred on the famous Porter scene, which transforms a mundane sound into a symbol of guilt, damnation, and the boundary between the moral and the demonic.

memes.label: Knocking
memes.description: The knocking motif in Macbeth — a recurring auditory image that marks the boundary between the secret world of murder and the public world of consequence.

memes.label: Lady Macbeth
memes.description: The wife of Macbeth and co-architect of Duncan's murder, whose iron will drives her husband to the throne before collapsing into guilt-maddened sleepwalking and apparent suicide.

memes.label: Lady Macbeth's Death
memes.description: 'The offstage death of Lady Macbeth in Act V, Scene V, reported to Macbeth and prompting his "Tomorrow, and tomorrow, and tomorrow" soliloquy (025-008).'

memes.label: Lady Macduff
memes.description: The wife of Macduff, murdered with her children on Macbeth's orders — a portrait of domestic innocence destroyed by political tyranny.

memes.label: Lennox
memes.description: A Scottish thane in Macbeth, present at key events from Duncan's murder to the final rebellion, whose shifting allegiance traces the arc of Macbeth's tyranny.

memes.label: Letter
memes.description: The letter as a dramatic device in Macbeth — Lady Macbeth's letter from her husband in Act I Scene v as the catalyst for ambition and a window into the Macbeths' marriage.

memes.label: Light (and Darkness)
memes.description: The light motif in Macbeth, tracing the play's movement from the extinguishing of natural light through the artificial lights of torches and candles to the final image of life as a "brief candle" — a trajectory that maps the collapse of moral visibility in Macbeth's Scotland.

memes.label: Loyalty
memes.description: The concept of loyalty in Shakespeare's Macbeth — the binding political virtue that Macbeth systematically betrays and that ultimately unravels his reign.

memes.label: Macbeth
memes.description: The tragic protagonist of Shakespeare's *Macbeth*, a Scottish nobleman driven by ambition and supernatural prophecy to murder King Duncan and seize the throne, descending into tyranny and madness.

memes.label: Macbeth's Castle
memes.description: Macbeth's castles at Inverness and Dunsinane — the domestic spaces of hospitality transformed into sites of murder, tyranny, and ultimately siege, mapping Macbeth's trajectory from honoured host to besieged tyrant. (005, 021-028)

memes.label: Macbeth's Coronation
memes.description: 'Macbeth''s coronation at Scone following Duncan''s murder, an event that occurs offstage but is reported in Act II, Scene IV of Shakespeare''s tragedy (011-014).'

memes.label: Macbeth's Death
memes.description: 'The death of Macbeth at the hands of Macduff in Act V, Scene VIII, fulfilling the witches'' final prophecy and ending the tyrant''s reign (028-012).'

memes.label: Macbeth's Investiture
memes.description: The ceremony at Scone in which Macbeth is crowned King of Scotland following Duncan's murder — a pivotal political moment that formalizes Macbeth's usurpation.

memes.label: Macbeth's Second Meeting with the Witches
memes.description: 'Macbeth''s visit to the Witches'' cavern in Act IV, Scene I, where they conjure the Apparitions whose equivocal prophecies give him false confidence and seal his fate (018-001).'

memes.label: Macdonwald
memes.description: 'A rebel against King Duncan in Shakespeare''s Macbeth, described as "merciless" and slain by Macbeth before the play begins (002-004).'

memes.label: Macduff
memes.description: The Thane of Fife who discovers Duncan's body, refuses to attend Macbeth's coronation, flees to England to join Malcolm, and ultimately kills Macbeth in fulfillment of the witches' prophecy.

memes.label: Macduff Kills Macbeth
memes.description: The climactic confrontation of Act V, Scene VIII in which Macduff kills Macbeth, fulfilling the witches' prophecy and restoring Malcolm to the Scottish throne.

memes.label: Macduff's Family's Slaughter
memes.description: 'The slaughter of Lady Macduff and her children by Macbeth''s assassins in Act IV, Scene II, the act that transforms Macduff into an implacable avenger (019-044).'

memes.label: Macduff's Son
memes.description: The unnamed young son of Macduff, murdered on Macbeth's orders in Act IV, Scene II — a witty, precocious child whose death marks the play's darkest turn into violence against the innocent.

memes.label: Madness
memes.description: Madness in Macbeth — from Lady Macbeth's warning that dwelling on deeds will make them mad, to Lady Macduff's charge that her husband's flight was madness, the play charts the psychological dissolution that follows moral violation. (009-021, 019-004)

memes.label: Malcolm
memes.description: Duncan's eldest son and heir to the Scottish throne, who flees to England after his father's murder, tests Macduff's loyalty, and returns with an English army to overthrow Macbeth and restore legitimate rule.

memes.label: Malcolm and Donalbain's Flight
memes.description: The flight of King Duncan's sons Malcolm and Donalbain from Scotland after their father's murder in Act II Scene iii, an act that casts suspicion upon them and clears Macbeth's path to the throne.

memes.label: Malcolm's Coronation
memes.description: The coronation of Malcolm as King of Scotland at the close of the play, restoring legitimate rule after Macbeth's tyranny and marking the play's return to moral and political order. (028)

memes.label: Malcolm's Return
memes.description: Malcolm's return to Scotland with the English army led by Siward and Macduff, marching from Birnam Wood to Dunsinane to challenge Macbeth's tyranny. (022, 024-028)

memes.label: Manhood
memes.description: The contested ideal of manhood in Macbeth, where characters repeatedly define, question, and weaponize masculinity as a goad to violence and a measure of moral worth.

memes.label: Masculinity in Macbeth
memes.description: A cross-entity analysis of how Macbeth interrogates masculinity — its definitions, its demands, and its destructive consequences. From Lady Macbeth's invocation to "unsex" herself to Macduff's redefinition of manhood through grief, the play exposes masculinity as a contested performance rather than a fixed essence.

memes.label: Menteith
memes.description: A Scottish thane who joins the rebel forces against Macbeth in Act V, questioning the tyrant's mental state and examining the psychology of guilt.

memes.label: Milk
memes.description: The milk motif in Macbeth traces a trajectory from nurturing compassion through poisoned maternity to universal concord, mapping the play's collapse of natural order onto the female body.

memes.label: Minion
memes.description: A term meaning "favourite" or "champion" that appears twice in *Macbeth*—first to describe the heroic Macbeth as "Valour's minion," then to describe the unnatural transformation of Duncan's horses into "minions of their race."

memes.label: Most Referenced
memes.description: A ranked index of the most cross-referenced pages in the Macbeth wiki, reflecting which characters, concepts, and events are most central to the play's interconnected world.

memes.label: Murder
memes.description: Murder as the central act of violence that drives the tragic action of Macbeth, encompassing both regicide and its cascading consequences.

memes.label: Nature (Unnatural)
memes.description: The central thematic opposition in Macbeth — the violation of nature and natural order by murder, usurpation, and witchcraft.

memes.label: Night
memes.description: The motif of night as a shroud for evil deeds, invoked by Macbeth and Lady Macbeth to conceal their crimes against the natural and political order in Shakespeare's Macbeth.

memes.label: Norway
memes.description: 'The Norwegian kingdom whose invasion of Scotland, led by King Sweno and assisted by the traitorous Thane of Cawdor, opens the military conflict in Shakespeare''s Macbeth (002-017).'

memes.label: Old Man
memes.description: An unnamed elderly character in Act II, Scene IV who discusses with Ross the unnatural portents following Duncan's murder, serving as a choric voice of moral and cosmic order.

memes.label: Omen
memes.description: Supernatural signs and portents in Macbeth that register nature's revolt against Duncan's murder and foreshadow the collapse of Macbeth's rule.

memes.label: Order (Political and Natural)
memes.description: The concept of natural and political order in Macbeth, rooted in the Elizabethan worldview of the Great Chain of Being, where the murder of a king disrupts both cosmic and social harmony.

memes.label: Order of Nature
memes.description: The hierarchical cosmic and political order that Macbeth's crimes overturn, producing unnatural phenomena — darkness at noon, horses eating each other — as nature's rebellion against regicide.

memes.label: Order vs Chaos
memes.description: The central structuring opposition in *Macbeth*—the cosmic, political, and psychological struggle between order and chaos that begins with Duncan's murder and is resolved, tentatively, by Malcolm's restoration.

memes.label: Organizations
memes.description: An index of all organizations, factions, armies, and groups in Shakespeare's Macbeth — from the Scottish thanes to the English forces and the Weird Sisters' coven.

memes.label: Owl
memes.description: The owl as a death omen in Macbeth — its shriek signals Duncan's murder, its unnatural predation mirrors the inverted cosmic order, and its howl marks the watch of night's darkest hours.

memes.label: Pity
memes.description: Pity personified as a cosmic force of exposure in Macbeth's pre-murder soliloquy, contrasted with the play's systematic suppression of compassion.

memes.label: Places
memes.description: A comprehensive index of all locations and settings in Shakespeare's Macbeth, from the Scottish heath to the English court.

memes.label: Power
memes.description: Power in Macbeth — a thematic exploration of legitimate versus usurped authority, the limits of mortal power, and the tyranny that corrupts those who seize power through violence. (018-036, 020-047, 021-016)

memes.label: Pray
memes.description: The concept of prayer in Macbeth — the act of addressing God that becomes impossible after regicide, marking the spiritual death that follows the murder of a divinely anointed king.

memes.label: Prayer
memes.description: The motif of prayer and its impossibility in Macbeth, marking the protagonist's severance from divine grace after Duncan's murder.

memes.label: Prophecies of the Witches
memes.description: 'The supernatural predictions delivered by the Weird Sisters that drive the plot of Macbeth — hailing Macbeth as Thane of Cawdor and King, and foretelling Banquo''s royal lineage (003-018).'

memes.label: Prophecy
memes.description: The engine of Macbeth's plot and its central philosophical problem — do prophecies reveal fate or create it? The Weird Sisters' predictions drive action while raising questions about free will, self-fulfillment, and the ethics of foreknowledge.

memes.label: Regicide
memes.description: The act of killing a king, presented in Macbeth as the supreme political and moral transgression that unravels both the individual soul and the cosmic order.

memes.label: Revenge
memes.description: Revenge as a driving force in Macbeth, from Macduff's personal vengeance to the play's broader exploration of retributive justice.

memes.label: Ross
memes.description: A Scottish thane and messenger in Macbeth who moves between opposing camps, delivering crucial news from the opening battle to the final victory.

memes.label: Scone
memes.description: The traditional coronation site of Scottish kings, where Macbeth and later Malcolm are crowned, marking the transfer of legitimate rule in the play.

memes.label: Scotland
memes.description: Scotland in Shakespeare's Macbeth.

memes.label: Serpent
memes.description: The serpent as a symbol of concealed evil in Macbeth, appearing in Lady Macbeth's instruction to "look like the innocent flower, but be the serpent under't" (005-034) and Macbeth's reference to Banquo as "the grown serpent" (015-014).

memes.label: Seyton
memes.description: Macbeth's loyal armor-bearer and attendant in Act V, whose name evokes Satan and whose presence underscores the infernal atmosphere of Macbeth's final hours.

memes.label: Shadow
memes.description: The shadow as metaphor for unreality, existential emptiness, and military deception in Macbeth.

memes.label: Sight (Vision and Blindness)
memes.description: The motif of sight and blindness in Macbeth, tracing the play's preoccupation with what can and cannot be seen — from the "sorry sight" of Duncan's corpse to the "horrible sight" of the apparitions and the "sightless" couriers of divine judgment.

memes.label: Sin
memes.description: The religious and moral conception of sin in Macbeth, and its entwinement with guilt and the loss of grace.

memes.label: Siward
memes.description: Siward, a character in Shakespeare's Macbeth.

memes.label: Siward's Death
memes.description: The death of Young Siward at Macbeth's hands during the Battle of Dunsinane — a moment of apparent victory for Macbeth that proves hollow.

memes.label: Sleep
memes.description: The motif of sleep in Macbeth—from "innocent sleep" murdered by regicide to the pathological sleepwalking of Lady Macbeth—tracing the play's central argument that the destruction of rightful kingship destroys the natural order, including the capacity to sleep.

memes.label: Sleep and Sleeplessness
memes.description: Sleep in Macbeth is never merely rest — it is innocence, peace, the balm of the wounded mind. Its systematic destruction across the play tracks the psychological cost of murder, from Macbeth's cry that he has "murdered sleep" to Lady Macbeth's somnambulistic confession.

memes.label: Soldier
memes.description: The figure of the soldier in Macbeth, representing competing models of martial identity — from the loyal warrior serving king and country to the hired murderer serving a tyrant's private will.

memes.label: Sorrow (Grief)
memes.description: The expression and suppression of sorrow in Macbeth — a play that interrogates whether authentic grief is possible when "false face must hide what the false heart doth know."

memes.label: Sovereign
memes.description: The concept of sovereignty in Macbeth — legitimate versus usurped rule, the divine sanction of kings, and the political chaos that follows when sovereignty is seized rather than inherited.

memes.label: Spirit
memes.description: The concept of spirits in Macbeth — supernatural agencies invoked or referenced by characters as sources of power, prophecy, and fatal knowledge.

memes.label: Spur
memes.description: The spur as metaphor for motivation and urgency in Macbeth, from Duncan's praise to Macbeth's self-diagnosis of inadequate ambition.

memes.label: Striking and Hesitation
memes.description: "A recurring motif in *Macbeth* connecting physical action to moral decision: to strike is to commit, and the inability to strike reflects the paralysis of conscience."

memes.label: Supernatural
memes.description: "A central thematic force in *Macbeth*: the witches' prophecies, Banquo's ghost, the apparitions, and the pervasive sense that nature itself rebels against Macbeth's crimes, together creating a world where the boundary between the natural and the unnatural collapses."

memes.label: Sword
memes.description: The sword as the instrument of honorable violence and its corruption in Macbeth — from Banquo's guarded blade to Macduff's sword as the voice of righteous vengeance.

memes.label: Tag Index
memes.description: A comprehensive index of all content tags in the Macbeth wiki, showing how concepts, characters, events, and places are categorized across thematic and structural dimensions.

memes.label: Temple
memes.description: The image of the temple in Macbeth — from the "temple-haunting martlet" at Inverness (006-004) to Duncan's murdered body as "the Lord's anointed temple" (010-028) — contrasting sacred architecture with violated sanctuary.

memes.label: Thane
memes.description: The Scottish feudal title borne by Macbeth, Banquo, Macduff, Ross, and other nobles—a rank that structures the play's political hierarchy and traces the arc of Macbeth's rise and fall.

memes.label: The Apparitions
memes.description: The three apparitions conjured by the Witches in Act IV Scene i, delivering cryptic prophecies that give Macbeth a false sense of invincibility.

relations.label: Macbeth's Second Meeting with the Witches

memes.label: The Battlements
memes.description: The battlements of Macbeth's castle — a threshold between the martial world of men and the supernatural realm invoked by Lady Macbeth, tracing an arc from triumph through invocation to siege. (002-004, 005-010)

memes.label: The Dagger Soliloquy
memes.description: Macbeth's famous "Is this a dagger which I see before me" soliloquy in Act II Scene i, in which he hallucinates a floating dagger leading him toward Duncan's chamber, marking the threshold between deliberation and regicide.

memes.label: The English Forces
memes.description: The army led by Malcolm, Macduff, and Siward that invades Scotland to overthrow Macbeth and restore the rightful monarchy.

memes.label: The Grooms' Murder
memes.description: Macbeth's killing of Duncan's two chamber grooms in Act II Scene iii, a pre-emptive act to silence witnesses and deflect suspicion after Duncan's murder.

memes.label: The Murderers
memes.description: Hired killers employed by Macbeth to murder Banquo, Fleance, and Macduff's family in Acts III and IV.

memes.label: The Opening Battle
memes.description: The opening battle of Shakespeare's Macbeth, in which Macbeth defeats the rebel Macdonwald and the Norwegian invasion, establishing his reputation as a valiant warrior before the audience meets him on stage.

memes.label: The Porter
memes.description: 'The drunken porter of Macbeth''s castle whose darkly comic scene provides the only moment of levity in the play, set against the discovery of Duncan''s murder (010-002).'

memes.label: The Porter's Scene
memes.description: The Porter's comic monologue at the opening of Act II Scene iii, where the drunken gatekeeper imagines himself the porter of hell-gate, providing the play's only sustained comic relief immediately after Duncan's murder.

memes.label: The Scottish Thanes
memes.description: The noble class of medieval Scotland who serve as regional lords under King Duncan and later shift allegiance during Macbeth's reign.

memes.label: The Sleepwalking Scene
memes.description: Act V, Scene I of Macbeth — Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking confession, observed by a Doctor and Gentlewoman, revealing her psychological collapse through compulsive hand-washing.

memes.label: The Weird Sisters
memes.description: The trio of prophetic witches whose predictions drive the plot of Macbeth, functioning as agents of fate under the goddess Hecate.

memes.label: The Witches
memes.description: 'The three Weird Sisters in Shakespeare''s Macbeth, supernatural agents whose prophecies drive the tragedy and embody the play''s themes of fate, equivocation, and moral chaos (001-001).'

memes.label: Themes
memes.description: A comprehensive index of major themes and motifs in Shakespeare's Macbeth, from ambition and guilt to the supernatural and natural order.

memes.label: Thunder (Supernatural Meteorology)
memes.description: The thunder that accompanies every witches' scene in Macbeth — supernatural meteorology as the external sign of moral and natural disorder.

memes.label: Time
memes.description: Macbeth's tortured relationship with time — his desperate attempt to control the future by murder, only to find that tomorrows "creep in this petty pace" toward meaninglessness.

memes.label: Toil
memes.description: The witches' refrain word "toil" in the cauldron scene, paired with "trouble," encoding the dark, productive labour of supernatural evil in Macbeth.

memes.label: Tomorrow (Existential Nihilism)
memes.description: Macbeth's "Tomorrow and tomorrow and tomorrow" soliloquy (V.v) crystallises the play's darkest philosophy — that human existence, stripped of meaning, is "a tale told by an idiot, full of sound and fury, signifying nothing."

memes.label: Tongue
memes.description: The tongue as the organ of speech and power in Macbeth — Lady Macbeth's rhetorical command, Macbeth's guilty silence, and the gendered politics of utterance.

memes.label: Traitor
memes.description: The political category of "traitor" in Macbeth, from the first Thane of Cawdor to Macbeth himself — a label that circulates until it swallows its speaker.

memes.label: Treason
memes.description: The breaking of political and personal loyalty in Macbeth, encompassing the betrayal of king, country, and comrades that defines the play's moral landscape.

memes.label: Trouble
memes.description: The concept of "trouble" in Macbeth as both the product of the witches' labour and the psychological affliction that consumes the guilty, from Duncan's murder to Lady Macbeth's sleepwalking.

memes.label: Trumpet
memes.description: The motif of trumpets in Macbeth, spanning from angelic "trumpet-tongued" judgment (007-002) to the "hideous trumpet" that announces Duncan's murder (010-043) and Macduff's final battle command (026-004).

memes.label: Tyranny
memes.description: The abuse of absolute power through illegitimate rule, embodied in Macbeth's kingship and contrasted with Duncan's divine-right monarchy.

memes.label: Tyrant
memes.description: The concept of tyranny in Shakespeare's Macbeth — the political label applied to Macbeth by his opponents as his rule descends into murderous despotism.

memes.label: Unnatural
memes.description: The concept of the unnatural in Macbeth — the cosmic and moral disruption caused by regicide, manifesting as inverted natural phenomena and psychological disorder.

memes.label: Valour (Military Courage)
memes.description: Military courage as the foundation and ultimate erosion of Macbeth's identity — from "Valour's minion" (I.ii) and "brave Macbeth" to the tyrant who "plays the humble host" while planning murder.

memes.label: Waiting-Gentlewoman
memes.description: A gentlewoman attending on Lady Macbeth who witnesses and reports her mistress's sleepwalking to the Scottish Doctor in Act V, Scene I.

memes.label: Watching and Being Watched
memes.description: "A recurring motif in *Macbeth*: characters watch, are watched, or fail to watch, reflecting the play's preoccupation with guilt, secrecy, and the impossibility of hiding from divine or human observation."

memes.label: Water
memes.description: Water as the symbolic medium of guilt and attempted cleansing in Macbeth, from Lady Macbeth's desperate "a little water clears us of this deed" to her compulsive hand-washing in the sleepwalking scene.

memes.label: Wine (Intoxication Motif)
memes.description: The motif of wine and intoxication in Macbeth, used as an instrument of deception, a source of false courage, and a metaphor for the draining of life and meaning.

memes.label: Witches (The Weird Sisters)
memes.description: The three witches (Weird Sisters) who prophesy Macbeth's rise and fall, embodying the play's supernatural machinery of fate and deception.

memes.label: Wood (Birnam Wood Motif)
memes.description: The symbolic motif of wood and forest in Macbeth, centred on the Birnam Wood prophecy that foretells Macbeth's downfall through an apparent impossibility made real.

memes.label: Worthy
memes.description: The concept of "worthy" in Macbeth — a term of honor and social recognition that becomes increasingly ironic as the title is applied to a man who murders his way to the throne.

memes.label: Wrath
memes.description: The concept of wrath in Macbeth — both human anger (Macbeth's tyrannical rage) and divine wrath (God's judgment on a sinful kingdom), distinct yet intertwined in the play's moral framework.

memes.label: Young Siward
memes.description: The son of Siward who fights and is killed by Macbeth in Act V, Scene VII. His death embodies the generational cost of tyranny and provides Macbeth with a false reaffirmation of the witches' prophecy before his final confrontation with Macduff.
